title: 2026/02/02 - PP (Part 2)
description: Monday, 2 February 2026 | Primary Production Racing Industry (Closure of Greyhound Racing Industry) Amendment Bill Hearing of Evidence: Allen Bryce Glenda Hughes Greyhound Racing New Zealand Edward Rennell, Chief Executive
descriptionSummary: Primary Production. Racing Industry (Closure of Greyhound Racing Industry) Amendment Bill. Allen Bryce

chunks.text: Thing says zoom. Is that, I'm gonna get that wrong. Oh, I was expecting to be on at five 20. Huh? Sorry, I was expecting to be on at five 20. Is my watch wrong? Nevermind. Um, thank you for the opportunity to talk to you. Um, I've been a veterinarian for 50 years.

Most of that time I've worked for, um, primary industry departments in different states and for the Commonwealth in, uh, Australia and, uh, in New Zealand for math and, and D-P-I-M-P-I rather, um, I've worked on animal welfare standards. Um, I've been, uh, part of, uh, national committees on animal welfare, particularly in Australia.

Um, I've been an expert witness in court cases, uh, dealing with, uh, animal cruelty. Um, I've been the independent chair of Greyhound Racing New Zealand's Animal Health and Welfare Committee for three years. Uh, the role of the Animal Health and Welfare Committee of GRNZ is to advance the care and wellbeing of racing greyhounds in New Zealand.

I don't represent GRNZ. I'm an independent, um, consultant and, uh, my role on the committee is as an the independent chair. I'm very proud of the achievements of, uh, the Animal Health and Welfare Committee in providing advice to GRNZ over that three year period.

And even before, uh, I, before the time that I was chair, when I became the chair, I met with the board. I told them that I was not prepared to do the job unless, uh, animal health, uh, sorry, animal welfare was, uh, central to all the decisions that, uh, that the board of GRNZ made.

And they gave me a commitment that they, that would be how it would work, and I have seen no evidence that they've, they've, uh, departed from that agreement. Over the three years, uh, I've seen GRNZ prioritize animal welfare at all times. There's been three reviews into the industry.

As you know, the first two were commissioned by the industry, and the third one, the Robertson Review in 20 20 21, uh, was commissioned by the government. Each of those highlighted issues with the industry and the, in each case, the industry responded to the issues that were, that were identified. The decision to ban the industry seems to have been based, uh, on the 2021 review findings with no recognition of the progress made by industry.

Since the Robertson Review was published, uh, there's been no contact between the minister and GRNZ over that period. Um, my sub, uh, my written submission, you'll find I list 23 initiatives that have been implemented since 2021 to improve grey and welfare.

For example, race fatalities have been reduced by 85%, and unnecessary euthanasia have been reduced to zero, and that zero has been for several years. Um, the previous government placed the industry on notice, as we all know, it had to improve greyhound welfare or be closed down.

There's no question that the industry did improve Greyhound welfare. In many ways, serious injury rates in New Zealand are comparable or lower than Australian Gray greyhound racing, and they're lower than in the, in both of the horse codes, either of the horse codes. Uh, race fatality numbers are roughly half of those for thoroughbred racing.

Even since the announcement of the closure in December, 2024, Greyhound racing, uh, greyhound welfare, sorry, has been improving. Injury severity is declining, and in the, the two most recent quarters, um, the, um, moderate to severe injury rates have been the lowest ever recorded.

The industry has been prevented from improving further, um, with the possible closure hanging over its head for three or four years. It could not invest millions in a new planned safer track at Ashburton, for example, to replace the Addington track in Christchurch. It was not possible for the, the board to say we're going to invest.

I think it was $9 million in, in, um, creating a new track, which would have been safer. Um, when the industry was under threat of closure, uh, or in new straight tracks, we built a new straight track in Wonga, we opened in 2023, and the injury rates, there are about half that of the curve tracks.

You've heard a lot about straight tracks already. I know, assuming so I'm, I'm against the closure, but assuming that closure will happen, fair compensation must be paid over a thousand people will be deprived of their livelihood directly. Plus, there's a, a lot of other indu, uh, businesses which depend on, um, income from the Greyhound racing industry.

Often the families that have been, that will be affected, have been in the business for generations. They're often in rural areas where their specialized skills are not readily translated into new employment. Compensation is not just a moral and ethical imperative. It's required by the government's own legislation. The Regi Regulatory Standards Act, section nine Part C, I've heard that, that the industry shouldn't be compensated because the industry's been on notice for, for three or four years.

Yes, it has been on notice, but it was under threat of closure if it did not address the issues raised in the reviews, and all the participants in the industry could see progress that had been made in regard to an to greyhound welfare. So the decision to close down was still of surprise to most of the industry, and it was a surprise to me.

I, I thought the industry had had done enough to be able to maintain its, uh, its, uh, existence. I, I'm just gonna say, Alan, before you carry on that, we've, we are just about at time. So if you want, and I know Miles Anderson has a question, so, um, if you want to wrap up now, we can take a question. Otherwise I will wrap up. I understood I had 10 minutes. Yeah.

So f so fair compensation must be paid if the industry is to close. The legislation should make this clear. Thank you. Okay, thank you. Um, miles, we've got a question. Yeah, thanks, chair. Um, Alan, we've had a number of submitters today talk about injury stats, um, saying that all of them are hidden, um, because they happen in training or off the track, so they're not included in the general statistics.

Could you, um, enlighten us, please? Uh, yeah, I think there's some truth in that, but I mean, there's, there's also an enormous amount of misinformation about, uh, statistics. Uh, we heard a minute ago that, um, what was the, the, the, uh, the figure was it 650 dogs were euthanized?

Um, that includes dogs that are, that were pets kept by, uh, member kept by trainers after they'd been re retired from the industry. And they might be 14 year olds who are euthanized, uh, as any pet dog might be euthanized at the end of its life. There's statistics and the statistics now, I agree that there are injuries that can happen off the track that might not come up in the official statistics.

Thank you. Excellent. Well, thank you very much for your time. Oh, have you got a quick question? Sorry, Mark's got a Question. Uh, thank you, chair. Uh, thank you for your submission. Um, right early on the piece, there was, um, an organization that made an allegation about, um, dogs being deliberately removed from public view, being warehoused to avoid scrutiny, um, et cetera.

There was no evidence provided. Have you got any thoughts on that? I didn't hear that. Um, submission, um, and I'm, I don't really know what, what, what it's about. Yeah, It was a, it's a written in, in their written submission. Oh, okay. Um, Greyhound dogs are currently being crated, substandard conditions deliberately removed and being warehoused, uh, to avoid scrutiny. Would You? Okay. Well, look, every, every, um, training premises is visited once a year, at least by, uh, the ROB inspectors.

And they're looking, they're, they're tracing every dog and ensuring that it's there. Yeah. Um, I can't see that that's gonna work. So that's not possible in your, Well, it would be difficult, and I don't believe it's happening. I can't say categorically. Thank you. Thank you very much for your time. Thank you.
We now have Glenda Cues in person for 10 minutes. Welcome, Glenda. Can I, before we start? Absolutely.

Good afternoon. As, uh, chair has just announced, I am Glen De Hughes. Um, I have previously served as an independent chair of the New Zealand Racing Board Chair of the Racing Integrity Board, interim Chief Executive Officer of Greyhounds New Zealand during the Robertson Review, and as a consultant to the SPCA, I currently chair Pet Refuge and am a director of Kids Can and Barnards.

In, uh, 2017, during my terms as the racing board chair, I initiated the Hanson Review as an independent, uh, organization from the Racing Board. So I guess it's a racing industry, initiated it, but not greyhounds. Um, and, and basically when that one came, and that was after the 2013 review, which was initiated by Greyhounds, which was the W-H-A-W-H-K review, I was then asked when I finished my term at the Racing Board, if, um, by the chair of Greyhound racing to serve as the interim CEO

with a clear mandate to lift Greyhound WA welfare. That mandate shaped every decision I made. And what I do want to say is, A, I'm not a punter and PB, um, I don't own greyhounds, and I've never been involved in the industry apart from understanding the importance of welfare, as you can tell by my current share pet refuge, based on observed outcomes arising from government and regulatory decisions, I have serious concerns about the racing industry closure of Greyhound Racing Industry Amendment Bill and its current film.

The bill lacks sufficient ev evidentiary support and for fails to ensure due process its passage will have significant negative consequences for livelihoods mental health, and will result in the extinction of a dog breed that has raced in New Zealand since the 18 hundreds. The bill is not a response to evidence.

It is a reaction to spin, and it does not achieve its stated purpose. You do not improve welfare by dismantling the regulatory system that enforces it. Removing this framework creates real risk, including the emergence of unregulated or black market racing. This concern was raised with members during the hearings last week.

This bill removes greyhound protections and lumps them with the general pet population, a sector or readily reported to be widely in crisis. This environment does not improve welfare. It shifts risks and obscures accountability. This matters because tens of thousands of companion animals are already being euthanized each year, and pounds and short and shelters often due to overcrowding and constrained resources.

No one knows this more than the SPCA, which no longer publicly shares its euthanasia, uh, numbers. Its head of advocacy actively lobbies government ministers to close greyhound racing. While the crisis in the companion animal sector, which struggles to reduce harm in euthanasia rates, is surely the country's more urgent problem.

Policy development should not be outsourced to advocacy organizations, however well intentioned they are. The regulatory impact statement acknowledges that the industry should have been consulted and was not. It explicitly identifies material risks to livelihoods and mental health, yet the bill fails to address them. I say this with respect for the Institution of Parliament.

This risks becoming an example of lawmaking divorced from consequences. The Rabbe Racing industry's new flagship welfare initiative is a purpose-built horse crematorium, which boasts the double burn chamber. Having personally overseen, world leading greyhound welfare reforms, transparency and lifetime traceability.

I find it jarring that these achievements are ignored while a horse crematorium is presented as life after racing. There is also a glaring double standard. Domestic greyhound racing is to be shut down. While Australian greyhound racing continues to be imported for gambling purposes, perversely profits from overseas greyhound racing will be used to fund the domestic force racing.

I added this in because members have asked why more track tracks were not built earlier. Under my leadership, the Wang Nu Track was closed, the original Wang Nu Track was closed. We then undertook extensive consultation with both local and international experts across multiple disciplines to ensure any straight track design genuinely reduced injury risk.

There was no point in just building something without the evidence and the research. This included curators, source specialists, veterinarians, engineers, lure drivers, trainers, and Professor David eager, a leading academic authority at a University of Sydney. Um, injury prevention and causation.

The straight track project was then delayed, not by lack of intent, but by land acquisition challenges, COVID, lockdowns and significant flooding Once land was secured and all expert advice reviewed construction of the Wgnu straight track commenced following its completion planning for a second straight track in the south island, which has already been discussed as as part of this, I want to, I, I'm sort of, I'm normally bought into fix systems.

That's, that's why people employ me. I've been employed in many organizations for doing that. Um, that's what you see with the pet refuge one and the kids can one or two examples of systems that I've actually worked on. So I would like to propose that a fair and workable middle ground might be worth considering. I found when I was dealing with the people in Greyhounds, they were extremely helpful and extremely responsive to all of the changes.

And some of those ones that I brought in were pretty tough, and I feel quite guilty about the mortgages that some of them have taken out to make sure that they were living and keeping up to the standard. Um, that, uh, that we were demanding targeted evidence-based reform, including limiting racing to one turn and straight tracks. There is also a lot less injuries on the bend of, of, uh, straight tracks as well, as long as the boxes are far away.

I never thought I'd be able to talk about stuff like this. I have to tell you, um, as well as where you place the starting box and all of those things to make a huge difference to injury. As a former athlete, I was able to understand that and, and it actually made real sense. So I just think including limiting racing to one turn and straight tracks would continue reducing the injuries, protect the livelihoods, and prevent the extinction of the braid breed.

People don't seem to realize that in about five or six years, there will be no greyhounds to wander around as a pet, and I won't be able to have one in my old age. It would also preserve the welfare gains already achieved and the industry substantial contribution to our economy if the ban proceeds.

Regardless, fair compensation must be provided to those who invested in good faith. Believe, just to ease my conscious a little bit, but for the, for the bigger reason, believing there would be proper and impartial process and the continued importation of overseas greyhound racing must end if that happens.

Fantastic. Thank, thank you very much. We have a question from Cameron Luxton, Uh, thank you chair. Um, thank you Glenda, for your submission. Um, there we've had the committee's heard, uh, some submitters say there is no, uh, way in which racing or exploiting animals that will ever be acceptable to them.

Do you feel that even if a compromise such as the one you were suggesting, if, if that was to happen, that that would ever alleviate the concerns of the people who want seemingly want to end greyhound racing? No. As, as a former of police officer, I'm extremely aware that there are people, and I admire them for them, and I accept their right to be there that are tied into a specific, sometimes ideology or a specific view.

So I don't think, I don't think whatever the the committee does with those people, that, um, you will change them and, and I accept that that's their right. Um, uh, do you have a sub on that, Cameron? No. Okay. Um, Tangi. Thank you Madam Chair. Um, thank you Glenda.

Uh, given y your background, uh, in this industry, one of the things that, uh, Greyhound Racing New Zealand have suggested is if this bill does proceed, that actually the role of the Mac is not best aligned to deal with transition, but Greyhound racing New Zealand itself would be, do you have any thoughts or views on the suitability of either, given the fact that you've been involved in a number of different, uh, sort of avenues and worn many hats? Yeah, Yeah. No, I, I certainly do.

Um, I have to admit that when I got into greyhounds and found out the whole complications of, and the importance of how those dogs are looked after, the fact that they are very different, um, the, the fact that the, the business is different, it definitely needs to be run by people that actually understand how it works and how the dogs work.

And I would definitely say that the current staff at Greyhound Racing New Zealand, although I must admit a conflict of interest here, 'cause I employed some of them, um, would be the right people to be actually doing that. And as for the, um, re-homing program, which had started before I got there, the people that are running that Rehoming program are just excellent and it would be absolutely crazy for it to go to anybody else.
Yep. Fantastic. Thank you very much and thank you for, um, taking the time to sit here all day and listen to everything. Yeah.

Okay. So last submitters of the day, Ray Hound Racing New Zealand. We have you for 20 minutes, so I'm sure there'll be plenty of questions, so please give us time for questions at the end. Thank you very much. We'll try and be short. Thank you Madam Chair, members of the committee. Thank you for the opportunity to appear before you today. My name is Edward Ronnell.

I'm the Chief executive of Greyhound Racing in New Zealand. With me today, I have Sean Hannon, our chairman, Dr. DA Gavin Gable, our animal welfare manager and Chief veterinarian and Sue Walls, our head of operations, and it as background. I've been fortunate to merge my interest in racing with my career for the majority of my professional life.

I've previously been the chief executive of harness racing in New Zealand for 21 years, chair of the T-A-B-N-Z dates Committee for three and general manager broadcast in operations for TABN NZ for two years. Since September 22. It has been my privilege to be chief executive at Greyhound Racing in New Zealand.

I have fond memories of going into my first Greyhound meeting as a kid in 1980 at the old KI E two park, taking GR NZ submission as read, I'll not waste your Time countering the misinformation, spurious allegations, and selective interpretation of statistics that many of the proponents of the band have outlined to you today.

JR Z's transparent reporting of statistics is subject to independent verification and monitored by the minister's statutory regulator, the Race Integrity Board. These statistics are independently verified and irrefutable. I've had a common question throughout the hearings is why didn't we do more?

The evolution, evolution of a progressive animal welfare program and the changes we have made over the last five years are significant revolutionary and frankly enormous. The Indi industry employs and consult experts and leads the way in reforms I can say with authority.

Given my extensive experience in the racing industry that greyhound racing leads the way in New Zealand in terms of animal welfare, I'm tremendously proud of our unrelenting commitment to do the right thing by our canine athletes. This commitment has been demonstrated to you in the voices and testimonies presented throughout the hearing.

Greyhound racing is the only racing code in New Zealand with full traceability of our animals. The only code where the animals are guaranteed a live post racing, and one that has a rehabilitation two rehoming scheme to ensure injured animals are medically cared for and subsequently adopted into suitable homes.

JNZ does not celebrate purpose-built Crematoriums as a wealth in wealth initiative. Our race day euthanasia rate is half that of the Thoroughbred Code in New Zealand. These hearings have been the first opportunity for our people to be heard by lawmakers, and I thank the members sincerely of this committee for the empathy that's been extended over the two days of hearing.

It's certainly been appreciated. Thank you. The fact that the minister responsible for this legislation can arrive at such a decision devastating people, dogs, and economic activity without, without any engagement with the industry, is an indictment on New Zealand's political processes.

It has made me and many question our faith in our political systems. You good people have the ability to restore that faith. Over the last two days, members have heard the harrowing and confronting submissions from our people. I don't, I do not need to repeat these.

I could never convey them as passionately or as eloquently as these genuine, hardworking new Zealanders that are impacted by this decision. The hurt, the utter devastation, the sense of unfairness and anger. The mental anguish was clear for all to see family units ripped apart.

Lifetime investments, obliterated generational commitments and passions. Wide doubt. You have to ask why to win a few more votes, secure more funding for the protected equine codes. I don't know. Only one man can answer that, and he refuses to talk to me. The legislation before you reflects the shoddy way.

The minister's office and the, and the officials have approached this. Legislate approached this decision. The ministerial advisory committee was appointed in December 24, and one of their key responsibilities stated in their terms of reference was to create a transitional plan for the closure of the industry.

14 months later, there is no plan and it is still work in progress. You and your parliamentary colleagues have been asked to pass a bill now and manage the consequences later. It's not good enough. Such plan, while it will be complex with a myriad of issues to be considered, has to be done, the bill is due to take effect in a matter of months.

Where is the plan? This is Max's responsibility, not GRNZ. This ongoing certainty as to what is going to happen in the very near future is causing great distress amongst our participants and negatively affecting the health and wellbeing of our people. It is a ban without a plan.

The process is absent due diligence, and I urge the committee to hold the minister to account on this. He is asleep at the wheel. If closure is to happen, which we are opposed to, it must be done in a fair and principled manner. At our first meeting with the ministerial advisory committee chair, Heather Simpson made it very clear that there would be no compensation for industry participants and that we had 18 months to scale down activity.

This was out with this without even having passed the bill. It is hard to escape the conclusion that the timing of the proposed closure was set more on the basis to occur just before the coming election, rather than what was logical in the best interest of the people and the dogs as outlined in our written submission, our participants have incurred costs and invested heavily in many cases, their life savings and good faith.

They have invested in breeding and racing greyhounds at considerable costs only to have careers curtailed by hasty and premature closure. They have invested in greyhound equipment and upgraded facilities to meet our comprehensive welfare standards. Those costs are now sunk and cannot be realized.

It is therefore heartening to hear members of this committee use the C word compensation. Thank you for this. Our preliminary estimates are that at least $40 million commitment would be required. We would welcome the opportunity, however, to provide 'em of more defined analysis once the government confirms its appetite for fairness and justice.

In looking at this, there is ample precedent in New Zealand law for such compensation. There are the key matters in our submission that require your consideration chiefly the absorption and wind up of legitimate incorporated societies. And the illogical double standard with the import of overseas greyhound racing.

The Green Party has already stated this is unacceptable, and I thank them and acknowledge this. Finally, Sue Bruce Robertson made the statement in his 2021 review that if closure was to be seriously contemplated, then a full scale inquiry will be required to assess all the consequences.

Why then has the industry not been extended this right? We would welcome such an inquiry surely before you wipe 159 million from the economy cost 1,054 hardworking people their jobs and destroy families and communities. You must be sure you are basing this on the right information, not prejudicial personal political agendas.

One wonders would Mr. Peters have been emboldened to make this decision if the SBCA hadn't offered him assurances, they would not be calling for a ban on horse racing. Personal convictions by the minister and his advisor, John Johansson, are not submission, are not sufficient to write laws. I implore this committee dedicated people who entered politics for the right reason to ignore party lines and make a statement, this style of lawmaking is not okay.

Please reject this bill and have the industry reviewed objectively and fairly. Thank you for your time and we welcomed your questions. Thank you very much. Thanks indeed. Um, so I have questions here from Miles, Cameron, Steve and Tongy in that order. Miles, you're up first. Thanks, chair. Look, um, thanks for your submission, Edward.

That was, um, uh, very fulsome. Could you, um, expand on what your, um, or what you believe is supposed to happen under the current proposal, um, to the Racing Club's assets? Well, the way the legislation is drafted, the assets would be, the clubs would be wound up by GRNZ if funds absorbed by G-N-G-R-N-Z and GRNZ would go through a similar process and, and the funds would transfer to the transition authority appointed under the act.

Uh, and, and has the ministerial advisory Committee engaged around this? We had some preliminary discussions, Sean and myself, I think back in May. But there has, we have had no more detail on that than what is provided in the draft legislation. We have had one couple of subsequent meetings in December, January around the preparation of the transition plan and some of it, and trying to identify the work that would be required in the closure.

And that's one aspect of it would need to be considered. Thank you. Um, we actually, we'll go to Tangi next. Thank you. Thank you Madam Chair. Um, thank you Edward. Um, I asked g Glenda Hughes about the, the placement of either Greyhound Race New Zealand or the Mac to, in the event that this bill does, uh, proceed deal with the transitional sort of phases.

Now you've alluded to this specifically in your written submission in terms of greyhound racing, New Zealand being best placed to lead out that particular phase. Just giving you an opportunity to perhaps elaborate a little bit more, um, in that space. Yeah, so we provided to the MAT committee an outline I can last April of how potentially REHOMING could be conducted, um, following the closure.

And what we were advocating that, uh, with that, um, is that essentially most of Greyhound racing structures could be maintained to manage that process along with, uh, our adoption a you know, the adoption agencies. Um, last year we, um, with the support of the agencies, we had 697 dogs adopted.

So that, you know, it was a significant achievement. It's not broken, it's working well. And we believe that those existing structures should be continued to drive that adoption process. It wouldn't be Greyhound racing New Zealand in its current form, but it would be, uh, it would need, you know, a a lesser version, but employing and utilizing the, the resources and assets of the people that are there.

Okay, thanks Cameron. Oh, thank you. Uh, uh, Tony touched on part of my question, but it's, do you think that whatever, uh, Edward, do you think whatever plan, uh, there has been a sufficient openness about a potential plan and based on your experience now with, uh, the ministerial advisory, uh, group or a committee, do you think that they will be able to rehome all the dogs?

No, it will take, you know, the REHOMING process. You know, when we looked at the papers on which the government made this decision, they were, the assumptions were that the whole population wouldn't be rehomed by 31 July 26. I think the Mac committee, when they started to review it, we quickly identified that that was very unlikely and almost impossible.

And I think their report indicates that it will take two to two and a half years post closure to achieve that. Uh, that in terms of, are they the best, um, people to drive that? Well, they were appointed in December 24. As of today, we don't have a transition plan.

They only advertise for a, um, establishment lead in December of 25. So for 12 months, I don't believe there has been sufficient progress. Thank you. And do your, oh, sorry. I'll leave it there. Chair. Thank you. Oh, thank you. Okay. Steve, do you have a new question?

Yeah, thank you. I do Madam Chair and thank you Edel. Good to see you all here and, um, appreciate you've been here for all of the hearings, um, in terms of understanding your core position. But, um, we, you usefully make key recommendations on how things should be done in your submission.

If there is to be a closure, you suggest that, uh, it be extended the closure date from the 31st of July 26th to July 29. Um, I wonder if it wasn't the full three years, but was one year or two years longer whether that would still be beneficial.

And my second part to that question is, is there a risk of its three years that, that then the breeding of new greyhounds would begin? Again? I think if there was to be a ban, but it was to be extended, there would need to be a ban on breeding so that we don't have more dogs coming in.

However, the three years was to ensure that dogs that have been bred can have a full, full career. Um, a dog generally starts racing at 18 months and on average they race through to up to four. So the extension would, you know, and we, we would've had pups born in January 25 that probably will not race before the band comes into effect.

That would bred in good faith. The key advantage in having an extension is you actually still provide the, you know, the benefits of trainers continuing to roast their dogs. There will be income coming in for the trainers and they can then look after their, their dogs that are in their kennels awaiting to go onto the great mate scheme.

So it provides the income for that to happen. If that doesn't happen and you just have no racing and you have dogs sitting in kennels held by TR trainers waiting to go into great mates, they will need to be reimbursed for that on a per day basis. 'cause they've got no income coming in from racing to feed the dogs, pay staff and ensure the welfare of those animals.

I just stop on that madam chair. Okay, I'll, I'll just go first if that's okay. Oh, you go. No, you go Steve, It, it's a quick stop on the finances of it because there has been a suggestion that if there is gonna continue to be, uh, income coming from the Australian gambling, that that should go to Greyhound racing New Zealand to pay for that sort of thing.

Wouldn't be that the, the other way to do it. Yeah, well, uh, effectively that's the income from that under the current funding arrangements, under the tri code funding agreement, the TAB makes a profit and makes a a, an allocation of three codes and they, they determine the split within, you know, Greyhound racing gets a share of that.

And market share is just around the 13 to 14%. I think our funding's just about 12 and a half. So the funding from that enables our ability to pay for our welfare initiatives, but also maintain and pay stakes and the like. But, but just to add to that, it's the, the income alone from Australian racing is insufficient to cover the cost to close the industry.

So to rehome two and a half thousand old greyhounds over the next three years, um, we estimate somewhere between 40 and $50 million. Um, if we were going to fairly and reasonably compensate participants, uh, then potentially there's, there's another 40 to $50 million, uh, you know, that someone's gotta pay for it.

Uh, and there's nothing in the bill that indicates who's gonna pay for it or how it's gonna be paid for. Uh, the income we get from the imported racing, uh, from Australia is, is roughly eight to 10 million a year. So there's a massive deficit that no one's quite comprehending quite yet.

Uh, and even if we did continue racing, uh, our market share would decline as the volume of dogs declines, the volume of racing will decline, our income will decline, but we've still got dogs to feed, dogs to be home, uh, and participant compensation to consider. Okay. I have a start going back to the previous line of questioning a a little bit about the, um, you talked about extending it and that would make it better for all the trainers and there was some people that raised the issue today about how the investment back into the track would be, would,

would wane as time went on when we knew it was gonna be a finite industry. So how would you ensure that that didn't then increase? How would you give reassurance to those people that were concerned about it? How would you give them reassurance that it wasn't actually gonna have a further detrimental impact on the safety of the dogs because of less investment because of the fine Industry? Yeah. Well, again, it depends on where the income comes from.

So currently we spend eight to 10 million a year on welfare initiatives. Um, track safety initiatives development. Uh, if, if our income is maintained at the current level, that investment can continue. Uh, but there will come a tipping point where if the income drops off, the money's gotta come from somewhere else. So someone has to pay for it.

Uh, and, and that's, that's the choice. You know, if the money's not there, you can't spend the money on either feeding the dogs or looking after the tracks or compensating the participants. All of which need to be done in a, in a, in a safe racing ecosystem. Thank you very much. Miles, you've still got your hand up?

Yeah, I've got another question Actually. Okay. Make it quick. Yeah. Look, we've been quoted, um, three different figures for the number of dogs that need to be rehomed. One figure is 1600, another figure is two and a half thousand, and the final figure is 3,300. Which figure, um, should we rely on?

Yeah, so our population miles were at the time of the decision was around 2,900. So when that happened, I think reading effectively stopped and our REHOMING has continued.
